coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived porous carbon with high drug adsorption capacity undergoes enzymatic and chemical degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Guohao
Zhang, Wenjuan
Du, Juan
Yuan, Xiaoling
Zhang, Wenxiang
Yan, Wenfu
Liu, Gang
coredata.dc:description:
Degradability is a key safety issue when choosing materials for biomedical applications and environmental protection. This factor greatly limits the application of porous carbon in these areas due to the inert and stable nature of carbon network. In this work, this conflict could be well-resolved by rational designing a mesoporous carbon (MC) with biomass as a carbon source. The retained oxygen-containing species simultaneously increase drug adsorption capacity and the degradability of MC. The maximum adsorption quantity for doxorubicin over MC can reach 395.3 mg/g, about 3-fold over carbon nanotubes. The detailed analysis reveals that the degradation of MC occurs via a radical mediated oxidation process. The high electron density feature of MC facilitates the electrophilic addition reaction in the presence of HO
. During this process, the carbon network is gradually degraded into fragments, carbon nanodots and ultimately to CO2. This work opens up a new way to fabricate degradable porous materials and provides a promising material for the practical application in biomedical and environmental field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous carbon
Biomass
Degradability
Adsorption

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enzymatic degradation of MC by HRP/H2O2
Chemical degradation of MC by H2O2
Synthesis and characterization of biomass-derived carbon
Degradation of MC under chemical conditions
Degradation of MC under enzymatic conditions
Mechanistic understanding the degradation of MC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis route used to prepare MC; (b) Raman spectra of MC; (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of MC, inset: corresponding pore-size distributions (Average pore diameter calculated using the BJH model based on the adsorption branch); (d) DRIFT spectra of MC; (e) high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s; (f) high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s; (g) Cytotoxicity study of different concentrations of the MC in L-929 cells by MTT assay.

(a) Adsorption kinetics of DOX on MC and carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) at 25 °C. (b) Adsorption isotherms of DOX by MC at different concentrations. The linear fitting curves of (c) Langmuir and (d) Freundlich models for the adsorption of DOX by MC at 25 °C.
(a) Visual appearance and colour changes of MC during degradation. TEM images of MC treated with H2O2 for (b) 0 h; (c) 40 h; (d) 70 h; and (e) 130 h.

Visual appearance and colour changes of (a) 5%Fe/MC; (b) 3%Fe/MC; (c) 1%Fe/MC and (d) MC during degradation. Reaction condition: the 50 mg sample was dispersed in low concentrations of H2O2 (99 mM, 100 mL) and reacted at 60 °C. The samples were taken every hour while 1 mL of hydrogen peroxide was added.
TEM images of MC treated with HRP for (a) 30 days and (b) 60 days; TEM images of MC treated with MPO for (c) 15 h and (d) 35 h.

(a) EPR spectra using DMPO as a HO
trapping agent in water; (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the supernatant of the sample after complete degradation; (c) The fluorescence spectra of the solutions with degradation time for 40 h, 60 h, 80 h, 100 h and 130 h, λex: 350 nm; (d) Schematic representation of MC degradation under chemical conditions.

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived porous carbon with high drug adsorption capacity undergoes enzymatic and chemical degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Surface and Interface Chemistry of Jilin Province, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Surface and Interface Chemistry of Jilin Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Surface and Interface Chemistry of Jilin Province, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering and Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mesoporous carbon derived from biomass exhibits high drug adsorption capacity and the degradability under both enzymatic and mild chemical conditions.

Degradability is a key safety issue when choosing materials for biomedical applications and environmental protection. This factor greatly limits the application of porous carbon in these areas due to the inert and stable nature of carbon network. In this work, this conflict could be well-resolved by rational designing a mesoporous carbon (MC) with biomass as a carbon source. The retained oxygen-containing species simultaneously increase drug adsorption capacity and the degradability of MC. The maximum adsorption quantity for doxorubicin over MC can reach 395.3 mg/g, about 3-fold over carbon nanotubes. The detailed analysis reveals that the degradation of MC occurs via a radical mediated oxidation process. The high electron density feature of MC facilitates the electrophilic addition reaction in the presence of HO
. During this process, the carbon network is gradually degraded into fragments, carbon nanodots and ultimately to CO2. This work opens up a new way to fabricate degradable porous materials and provides a promising material for the practical application in biomedical and environmental field.

ce:section.ce:para: Porous carbon is one of the most attractive materials, which is widely used in the fields of catalysis, energy, adsorption, electronics and biomedicine et al. [1–9]. Generally, elemental carbon of porous carbon occurs in the mixed allotropic forms of sp2 and sp3 hybridization [10]. Such configuration endows the porous carbon with high mechanical and chemical stability, which is resistant to degradation under most of acidic and alkali conditions [5,10–14]. These characteristics are desirable for materials to be used as drug carriers, catalyst supports, adsorbents et al. [13–18]. But at the same time, the high stability has induced long-term latent risk. The increasing production of porous carbon-based materials into biological systems has raised fundamental issues on their impact on human health and environment [19–21]. Especially for biomedical applications, the inert nature makes it problematic for clearance from the body and ultimately causes bioaccumulation [20,22,23].

To reduce the potential hazards of these uses, some efforts have been devoted to increase the degradable nature of porous carbon [24,25]. Current strategies include surface functionalization and defect engineering. On the one side, oxygen functional groups could improve the dispersibility of carbon materials in aqueous media [26–29]. Additionally, there is some evidence representing that the incorporated oxygen functionalities and/or defects could improve the accessibility to the active sites of enzyme [29–32]. Moreover, from the view of catalysis, our previous work has demonstrated that oxygen functional groups, especially carboxylic groups, facilitate oxygen activation and form active oxygen species for participating oxidation reactions [33]. However, the main challenge around this topic is how to maximize the surface functionality of carbon materials.

Up to now, two-dimensional (2D) graphene is the most easily functionalized carbon-based material [26,34]. Typically, tertiary alcohols and epoxy functional groups are attached to the basal plane and carboxylic acid, keto, and 5- and 6-membered lactol groups decorate the edge [10,35]. As for three-dimensional (3D) carbon (e.g. carbon nanotube, mesoporous carbon), limited oxygen-containing groups were obtained at edge defects of the basal plane. In contrast, three-dimensional carbon is more difficult to be degraded even under relatively harsh conditions [25,36,37]. However, the pore structure of 3D carbon materials has clear advantage in many applications, especially in constructing efficient drug delivery systems [38–41]. Therefore, enhancing the degradability of porous carbon is an essential precondition to extend their applications.

In this work, a kind of porous carbon combined with high drug adsorption capacity and degradability was obtained, in which biomass was used as carbon precursors. Biomass represents an abundant carbon–neutral renewable resource for the production of materials. Rich in oxygen element is one of the characteristics of biomass. Here, it is demonstrated that desirable oxygen-containing groups could be retained in resultant materials, leading to a high drug adsorption and degradable properties. A series of characterizations represent that the degradation of MC occurs via a radical mediated oxidation process. The high electron density feature of MC facilitates the electrophilic addition reaction in the presence of HO
. The carbon network is gradually degraded into fragments, carbon nanodots and ultimately to CO2.

Phosphoric acid (P, 85%), aluminium nitrate nonahydrate (98%), citric acid (CA, 98%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), nitric acid (HNO3), NaCl, NaH2PO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4·2H2O were from Alfa Aesar. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT), isopropyl alcohol (≥99.5%), pancreatin and streptomycin were from Sigma Aldrich. Mouse fibroblast cell (L-929 cell) were from the American Strain Preservation Center. Multi-wall Carbon Nanotubes (98%) with outer diameter of <8 nm and a length of 10–30 µm were obtained from Beijing Deke Daojin Technology Co., LTD. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX, 97%), 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), sucrose (SU, ≥99.9%), ammonium hydroxide (25%), Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (99%), Na2CO3 (99.99%), NaHCO3(≥99.8%) and NaOH were from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), human myeloperoxidase (hMPO), diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid (DPTA) and phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) were from Shanghai Guchen Biotechnology Co., LTD. All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade, and were used without further purification. Double-distilled water was used in all of the experiments.

The mesoporous carbon (MC) was prepared using a modified hard-template method, reported by our group previously [33]. Typically, phosphoric acid was dropped into the aqueous solution containing aluminium nitrate nonahydrate, citric acid (denoted as CA) and sucrose (denoted as SU) with specific mole ratio (CA:SU:Al:P = 0.5:0.5:1:1). Then, adjust the pH value of the solution to 5.1 with a diluted ammonia (10%) solution. After that, the mixed solution was evaporated at 80 °C to obtain the as-synthesized precursor. The precursor was first pretreated at 300 °C for 10 min in muffle, and then calcined at 800 °C for 6 h under argon atmosphere. Lastly, the composite was treated with 4 M HNO3 at 80 °C for 6 h to remove the AlPO component. The resulting products were filtered, washed with deionized water, and dried at 100 °C overnight. 1%, 3% and 5% iron were loaded on MC by wet impregnation method (abbreviated as 1% Fe/MC, 3% Fe/MC and 5% Fe/MC). Typically, MC was dispersed in the aqueous solution containing a certain amount of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, and then stirred at room temperature for about 3 h. After that, the solution was evaporated at 80 °C, and the resulting powder was calcined at 400 °C under Ar flow for 6 h.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were measured at 77 K, using a Micromeritics ASAP 2010 N analyzer. Samples were degassed at 473 K for 20 h before measurements. Specific surface areas were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model. Pore size distributions were evaluated from adsorption branches of nitrogen isotherms using the Barret-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCA LAB 250 system with MgKα source (1254.6 eV). Diffused reflectance of infrared Fourier transform (DRIFT) spectra were recorded on Thermo Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a Bruker RFS 100 Raman spectrometer with an argon laser (514 nm) as an excitation source. The fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (F-2500, Hitachi, Japan) with a xenon lamp source (The excitation wavelength was 350 nm). The supernatant of samples at different time periods was diluted 5 times. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a diode array spectrophotometer (UV-2102PC, Unico (Shanghai) China) with a deuterium lamp source. The supernatant of samples was diluted 5 times. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) data was obtained using an electron spin (paramagnetic) resonance spectrometer CMS-8400 from ADANI, Minsk, Belarus. The operating conditions were as following: magnetic field 337.0 ± 5.0 mT width, power attenuation 10 dB, field modulation 0.100 mT, sweep time 100 s, microwave frequency 9450 MHz. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with a FEI Tecnai F20EM with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV equipped. Boehm titration method was used to determine the amount of surface groups. Mesoporous carbon (0.1 g) was dispersed in 10 mL of 0.05 M solution of the following: sodium hydroxide, sodium carbonate, sodium bicarbonate. The solution was stirred for 24 h and filtered, and then a known amount of hydrochloric acid was added to each filtrate. The excess acid left in the solution was titrated with NaOH on an Automatic Potentiometer titrator (LeiCi ZDT-4A). The amount of acidic sites was calculated with the assumption that NaOH neutralizes carboxylic, phenolic, and lactonic groups, NaHCO3 neutralizes carboxylic groups, and Na2CO3 neutralizes carboxylic and lactonic groups.

The thiazolyl blue tetrazolium Bromide (MTT) assay was employed to investigate the cytotoxicity of MC, taking mouse fibroblast cell (L-929 cell) as cell lines. L-929 cells were cultured in MEM medium containing 10% embryonic serum and antibiotics (penicillin 100 U/mL and streptomycin 100 μg/mL) and placed in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C. The cells were digested with trypsin to prepare cell suspension. After centrifugation (200 g, 3 min), the cells were re-dispersed in fresh medium, and the cell density was adjusted to 1 × 105 cells/mL cell suspension. The cells were inoculated into 96 well culture plates, 100 μL per well, and placed in a carbon dioxide incubator (5% CO2, 37 °C, humidity greater than 90%) for 24 h. After the cells grew into monolayer, the original medium was sucked out, and 100 μL of different concentrations of test sample extract (100%, 75%, 50%, 25%), blank control solution, positive control solution (100%) and negative control solution (100%) were added, respectively, and cultured at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h (6 parallel samples). After culture for 24 h, the 96-well plates were taken out and observed under the microscope for cell morphology. Then, the liquid was desorbed and 50 μL MTT (1 mg/mL) was added to each well. The supernatant was desorbed and 100 μL isopropyl alcohol was added to each well to dissolve crystallization. The optical density was measured at 570 nm on the microplate oscillator for 10 min. The following formula was used to calculate the viability of cell growth: Viability (%) = (mean of absorbance value of treatment group/mean absorbance value of control) × 100.

The adsorption isotherms of DOX on MC were achieved with 5 mg of MC in 25 mL of DOX aqueous solutions (80 mg/L, 100 mg/L, 120 mg/L, 150 mg/L, 200 mg/L) at 298 K, followed by stirring for 720 min. Then, MC was separated from the suspension via filtration. The concentration of DOX was determined by the spectrophotometric standard curve method. The adsorbed amount of drug per unit weight of MC (mg/g) at equilibrium was calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
q e
= V
(
C 0
-
C e
)
/ m
where m (g) is the mass of MC, V (mL) is the total solution volume, C 0 and Ce (mg/mL) represent the initial and equilibrium concentrations of DOX, respectively.

The kinetic curve of DOX on MC (or carbon nanotubes) was determined with 50 mg of MC (or carbon nanotubes) in 250 mL of DOX aqueous solutions (100 mg/L) at 25 °C. Next, taking 3 mL of the samples regularly at different intervals. The concentration of DOX was determined by the spectrophotometric standard curve method. In order to evaluate the effect of contact time on the amount of DOX adsorbed on MC (or carbon nanotubes), the adsorption capacity at different time was calculated according to Eq. (2):
(2)
q t
= V
(
C 0
-
C t
)
/ m
where m (g) is the mass of adsorbent, V (mL) is the total solution volume, and C0 and Ct (mg/mL) represent the initial and concentrations of DOX at different time. The concentrations of DOX were determined by means UV–Vis spectrometer Shimadzu 2401 (Shimadzu Enterprise Management Co., Ltd., Shimadzu, Japan) at λmax = 480 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzymatic degradation of MC by HRP/H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed experimental steps are based on a procedure reported by Kurapati et al. [60]. The suspension of 0.2 mL of MC aqueous solution (stock concentration: 0.78 mg/mL) was added to 0.8 mL PBS and sonicated in an ultra-sonication bath for 2 min. Then, 1.0 mL of HRP (1.1 mg) pre-solubilized in PBS was added to the suspension. To enable the enzymatic activity of HRP, 2.0 µL of 50 mM H2O2 was added once every day up to 60 days. All suspensions were kept in the dark at room temperature and stirred for the entire duration of the experiment. HRP was refreshed every 20 days (adding 1.0 mg of HRP dissolved in 0.4 mL of PBS). Aliquots (50 µL) were taken at time points 0, 7, 12, 21, 30, 45 and 60 days and stored at −20 °C in the dark until characterization by different techniques.

The detailed experimental steps are based on a procedure reported previously [60]. The suspension of 32 µL of MC (stock concentration: 0.78 mg/mL) was added to 193 µL of 50 mM phosphate buffer containing 140 mM NaCl and 100 µM DTPA. For this suspension, 25 µL of hMPO (1 µg/µL) pre-dissolved in 50 mM phosphate buffer were added. Then, 200 µM concentration of H2O2 was added (5 µL of 10 mM of H2O2) every hour for 35 h in total. hMPO was renewed every 5 h. The reaction mixture was maintained at 37 °C in the incubator. The control experiments involving addition of only H2O2 or hMPO, were also performed using the same protocol. Aliquots (25 µL) were taken at time points 0, 15, 24 and 35 h, and stored at −20 °C in the dark room until their characterization by different techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical degradation of MC by H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: MC (50 mg) was firstly dispersed in low concentrations of H2O2 (99 mM, 100 mL) under stirring. The device was kept in water bath at 60 °C. 2 mL of sample was analyzed after 5 h (or 1 h) reaction and additional H2O2 was supplemented to keep the environment same as the initial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of biomass-derived carbon
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of porous carbon used a modified hard-template method, which was reported by our group previously [42,43]. Fig. 1 a illustrates the main procedures of the synthesis. All the precursors, including citric acid, sugar, aluminum and phosphorus ions, were homogeneously mixed in aqueous solution. Subsequently, an organic–inorganic composite was obtained by evaporating water. In-situ formed aluminophosphate (AlPO) in the process of carbonization was used as inorganic template. After removing AlPO, porous carbon (denoted as MC) with biomass as carbon sources was obtained.

Fig. 1b–f and supplementary Fig. S1, S2 and S9 represent the structure and surface properties of MC. XRD pattern and Raman spectrum show that the framework of MC is with relatively low degree of graphitization (Fig. S1 and Fig. 1b). The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm (Type IV) and the pore size distribution show that MC possesses mesoporous characters with a specific surface area of 1392 m2/g (Fig. 1c and Table S1). The average pore size and pore volume are about 6.8 nm and 1.51 cm3/g, respectively (Table S1). TEM image further confirms the presence of meso-scale pores in MC (Fig. 1a and Fig. S2). Such features are desirable as a candidate to construct a drug delivery system.

A large number of functional groups (including carboxylic, lactonic and phenolic groups) were detected on the surface of MC. In DRIFT spectrum of MC, two signals with strong intensity at 1630 cm−1 and at 1350 cm−1 can be observed, which can be assigned to CO and CO stretch vibrations (Fig. 1d) [44–46]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to detect the chemical states of C and O element on the surface of MC. The corresponding C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks with the binding energy centered at 284.7, 285.84, 286.8 and 289.25 eV (Fig. 1e), which respectively represent CC, CC/CO, CO/OCO and O-CO [41]. The high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 1f) can be deconvoluted into three peaks with the binding energies of 531.03, 532.57 and 533.92 eV that correspond to quinone, OC, and COH [43,47]. Boehm titration was carried out to quantitatively analyze the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of MC. The amount of carboxylic, lactonic and phenolic groups are 0.92 mmol/g, 0.91 mmol/g and 1.40 mmol/g, respectively (Table S2). On one hand, the formation of a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups can be ascribed to the abundant oxygen element in the biomass carbon source. Besides, the in-situ formed AlPO template effectively protects the oxygen species. Our previous work demonstrated that the interface of AlPO/carbon can be regarded as the cradle for the formation of oxygen-containing functional groups [33,40].

Biocompatibility is a basic and vital factor for the materials to be applied in the biomedical field [48,49]. In this work, L-929 cells were used as the model to investigate the potential cytotoxicity of MC. Fig. 1g shows the cell viability of L-929 in different concentration extracts of MC. The cell viability remains 92% after incubation for 24 h in the presence of 25% MC extractions. Even cultured in the high concentration, the cell viability of L-929 cells still remains over 80%. According to the standard of ISO10993-5-09, cell viability of 70% is an important value to evaluate the toxicity of the materials. In our case, the obtained results show that MC is of excellent biocompatibility.

For evaluating the potential application of MC in biomedical field, doxorubicin (DOX) was chosen as a model to investigate the adsorption behavior of MC. DOX is a cytotoxic antineoplastic antibiotic, using in the therapy against several cancers [50,51]. Like many cytotoxic drugs, the disadvantage of non-specificity requires searching for efficient carriers to transport the drug molecules to areas need treatment [52,53]. Fig. 2 a shows the adsorption kinetics curve of DOX over MC with initial concentration of 100 mg/L. It should be noted that DOX is stable in the aqueous solution at room temperatures (Fig. S3). So, the adsorption quantity could be investigated by detecting the concentration of DOX in the solution. Fig. 2a shows that the adsorption quantity (qt) of MC increases quickly with time increasing, which could reach 48% of its equilibrium uptake in the first 10 min. After 600 min, the adsorption process approached the equilibrium. The maximum of test qt is 305.2 mg/g. In contrast, the adsorption quantity (qt) of carbon nanotube could only reach 127.7 mg/g, much lower than that of MC.

In addition, the adsorption quantity (qe) of MC at equilibrium adsorption capacity was investigated at different initial concentrations (80, 100, 120, 150 and 200 mg/L). The corresponding results were shown in Fig. 2b. These experiment data were further processed with Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms. Generally, the Langmuir model is based on the assumption that the process of adsorption takes place on a homogenous surface endowed with equivalent adsorption sites, while the Freundlich model is based on adsorption on a heterogeneous surface or microporous surface [54,55]. Fig. 2c and d shows linear fitting course of Langmuir and Freundlich. The correlation coefficients calculated based on the Langmuir model (R2 = 0.9996) and the Freundlich model (R2 = 0.9797) confirm the homogenous distribution of active sites, that is, the Langmuir model well represents the nature of adsorption process. According to this model, the maximum adsorption capacity is 395.3 mg/g well corresponds to the experimental value of 374.3 mg/g (Fig. 2b). These results represent that the biomass-derived MC can serve as a high-efficient adsorbent for anticancer drugs.

It is well-known that oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of adsorbent materials play an important role in the adsorption process [56–59]. In order to verify the role of oxygen-containing functional on MC surface, the MC was calcined at 800 °C under argon atmosphere for 6 h (denoted as MC-800) to remove the surface functional groups. As shown in Table S4, the Boehm titration result showed that the number of the surface oxygen-containing functional groups of MC-800 decrease in comparison with MC. Correspondingly, the adsorption capacity is significantly reduced (Fig. S4). This result confirms that the adsorption capacity of MC correlates with the amount of surface functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of MC under chemical conditions
ce:section.ce:para: MC (50 mg) was firstly dispersed in low concentrations of H2O2 (99 mM, 100 mL) under stirring. The device was kept in 60 °C constant temperature water bath. 2 mL of sample was analyzed after 5 h reaction and additional H2O2 was supplemented to keep the environment like the initial. Fig. 3 a shows the samples over a course of 130 h. This reaction significantly improves the solubility of MC in aqueous media at the initial stage (sample 1 to sample 12). These suspended carbon materials could remain well dispersed at room temperature and no precipitation can be observed until we completed this manuscript (more than 10 months). Subsequently, the suspended carbon materials were decomposed and a clear solution was finally formed. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out to track the important degradation stage of MC. As observed in Fig. 3b–e, the porous structure of MC was gradually destroyed with the increase of reaction time. Finally, only small amount of carbon dots with the size of 10 nm could be observed (Fig. 3e). All these results show that MC could be degraded under above chemical conditions. In contrast, the structure of conventional active carbon and nanotube is basically unchanged under similar conditions.

The degradation of MC could be accelerated in the presence of Fe ions. We separately loaded 1%, 3% and 5% Fe on the surface of MC. The complete degradation time was shorten to 14 h, 10 h and 8 h, respectively (Fig. 4 ). It is well-known that Fe2+ ions and H2O2 are two main reagents of Fenton method. Fenton oxidation process is widely used in industrial wastewater treatment. The high efficiency generally ascribes to the formation of HO
. In our case, the acceleration of degradation rate through introducing Fe ions should mainly due to the formation of HO
. All these results indicate that MC could be easily degraded in the presence of HO
. The detailed investigation on mechanism would be discussed in the later section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of MC under enzymatic conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Two different routes were chosen to investigate the biodegradability of MC. The treating condition refers to the biodegradation of hexagonal boron nitride [60]. Firstly, MC was treated with the plant enzyme HRP. It is known that HRP is a model peroxidase enzyme that generates highly oxidative intermediates. To enable the enzymatic activity of HRP, 2.0 µL of 50 mM H2O2 was added once a day up to 60 days. All suspensions were kept in the dark at room temperature and stirred for the entire duration of the experiment. Fig. 5 a and b shows the TEM images of treated MC after 30 and 60 days. Similar to that under chemical conditions, MC materials undergo crushing and eventually form small fragments.

Secondly, aqueous dispersion of MC was treated with human MPO in the presence of NaCl and H2O2. In this case, hydrogen peroxide was added every hour for 35 h. Fig. 5c and d shows the TEM images of treated MC after 15 h and 35 h. In comparison with the plant enzyme HRP, MPO is more suitable for the degradation of MC. After 35 h treatment, porous MC materials have been degraded to small fragments. This degradation rate is obviously higher than that of hexagonal boron nitride [56]. Combined all these results, it can be concluded that MC could be degraded under both chemical and enzymatic conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic understanding the degradation of MC
ce:section.ce:para:
Obviously, the degradation of MC under both chemical and enzymatic conditions occurs via an oxidation process. It is very similar to the process of Fenton or Fenton-like catalysis, in which, homolytic cleavage of H2O2 to form hydroperoxyl and hydroxyl radicals is the predominant pathway [61]. Different from conventional Fenton catalysis, the transform of MC could occur in the absence of ferrous salts or enzyme containing metal ions. Fig. 6 a shows the EPR spectrum obtained in the presence of DMPO, H2O2 and MC. The spectrum was composed of quartet lines having a peak height ratio of 1:2:2:1 and the parameters were hyperfine constants αN = 1.49 mT, αH = 1.49 mT and g-value = 2.0055. These parameters coincided with those of the DMPO-OH adduct as reported in literatures [62]. The results suggest that HO
could form under our metal-free conditions. In addition, tert-butanol was used as a hydroxyl radical inhibitor to verify the critical role of HO
. Under similar conditions, the presence of tert-butanol inhibited the degradation of MC (Fig. S5). This result confirm the formation of HO
in the system containing MC and H2O2. MC was degraded by these in-situ formed HO
.

It is well-known that HO
is an active oxidant with a high oxidation potential. In our case, CO2 gas was measured as a final product of the degradation process (Fig. S6). A series of characterizations were carried out to detect the intermediate species resulting from incomplete degradation. The UV–vis absorption spectrum showed an intensive absorption band in the range of 260–300 nm (Fig. 6b). It resulted from the π-π* transition of CO [63,64]. According to the literatures, this signal could indicate the generation of carbon dots [63,64]. From TEM images, carbon nanodots with a diameter about 10 nm can be observed clearly. Photoluminescence (PL) was carried out to measure the emission spectrum of the mixture at different reaction periods (Fig. 6c). It can be observed that a broad band with a maximum at 480 nm can be observed upon excitation at 350 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the solutions increased with increasing degradation time demonstrating an increase in the total number of carbon dots.

HO
can attack the high electron density sites as an electrophilic reagent. Electrophilic addition reaction is easily carried out by HO
over the unsaturated substances. It is known that MC is assemblies of concentric sp2 bonded carbon, abounding with plentiful π-bonding electrons in carbon framework. During the degradation, HO
can attack the bonds of CC and the defect sites on the surface of MC, forming new oxygen-containing groups on the surface of MC (see Fig. S7). These oxygen-containing groups were further oxidized by HO
forming CO2 and H2O. During this process, the porous structure of MC was gradually degraded into fragments, carbon nanodots and CO2 (Fig. 6d).

It should be noted that MC is much more easy to be decomposed in comparison with traditional active carbon (AC) and carbon nanotube (MWCNTs) et al [21,25,31]. Under the same conditions, little change can be observed over AC and MWCNTs during 40 h investigation (Fig. S8). According to above analysis, the advantage of MC is the highly developed porous structure and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. These features facilitate to improve the adsorption capacity of drug as well as accessibility of H2O2 and enzyme on the surface of carbon. In addition, the electron donation feature of MC is another advantage for the degradation. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrum shows that MC represents a signal at g factor of 2.00 (Fig. S9). Since the measurement is carried out under ambient condition, this signal should be ascribed to surface superoxide radical anion
O2 – species. This result could reflect the electron donation performance of MC. It can well explain that MC could drive the cleavage of H2O2 to form HO
species.

Porous carbon derived from biomass is demonstrated an efficient strategy to prepare degradable carbon-based materials for drug adsorption. Retaining the oxygen-containing species in the resultant carbon is a critical factor to increase the adsorption capacity and the degradability of MC under both enzymatic and mild chemical conditions. The degradation of MC occurs via a radical mediated oxidation process, in which HO
attacks MC with high electron density properties. The carbon network is gradually degraded into fragments, carbon nanodots and ultimately to CO2. This strategy could be extended to other biomass as carbon source, which could provide a series of promising materials for the application in biomedical and environmental field.

Guohao Xu: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wenjuan Zhang: Project administration, Formal analysis, Resources. Juan Du: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Xiaoling Yuan: Project administration, Software. Wenxiang Zhang: Formal analysis, Methodology. Wenfu Yan: Methodology. Gang Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (22072054 and 21972053), the Development Project of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (20170101171JC, 20180201068SF), the Open Project of State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry (202105) and the Open Project Program of Key Laboratory of Preparation and Application of Environmental Friendly Materials (Jilin Normal University), Ministry of Education, China (No.2020009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.04.064.
